hook: Six free floors devoted entirely to parasites, crowned by a tapeworm hung beside a ribbon for scale
facts: Founded in 1953 by Dr Satoru Kamegai from samples collected in the post-war years
Holds around 45,000 parasite specimens; roughly 300 are on display
Star exhibit: an 8.8-metre tapeworm removed whole from one man
Admission is free; it is one of few museums of its kind in the world
w: Dr Satoru Kamegai began collecting parasites from patients in the unsanitary years after the war, and by 1953 had enough to open a museum to them. Six floors of jars later, the star exhibit reaching nearly nine metres: a tapeworm pulled whole from one man, hung beside a ribbon so you can pace its length. Free, and quietly horrifying.

hook: A 10th-century rebel's head mound that survived skyscrapers because clearing it kept killing people
facts: Said to hold the head of Taira no Masakado, beheaded in 940 after rebelling against the court
Sits on some of the most valuable land in Tokyo, facing the Imperial Palace
After the 1923 quake the Finance Ministry's planned building was abandoned following a string of deaths
Designated a historic site by the Tokyo Metropolitan Government and tended to this day
legend: Legend says his severed head flew east from Kyoto to find its body and landed at this spot
Workers and officials who disturbed the mound are said to have died mysteriously
w: Beheaded in 940 for declaring himself emperor, the rebel Taira no Masakado had his head displayed in Kyoto, where legend says it flew east to find its body. It landed here. When the ministry tried clearing the mound after the 1923 quake, officials kept dying; they gave up. Salarymen still bow to it daily.

hook: The temple that claims to have birthed the lucky beckoning cat, now buried under thousands of them
facts: Linked to Lord Ii Naotaka of the Hikone domain in the early 17th century
Now displays well over a thousand white maneki-neko, left by visitors and constantly multiplying
The raised right paw is said to beckon wealth and good fortune
Set in quiet residential Setagaya, well off the central tourist trail
legend: Legend says a temple cat raised its paw to beckon a passing lord inside, sparing him from a lightning bolt that struck the spot he had left
w: Legend says a passing lord sheltered here from a storm after a temple cat raised its paw to beckon him in, sparing him a lightning strike that split a nearby tree. Grateful, he made the poor temple his clan's. Now thousands of white beckoning cats crowd the shelves, ears cocked. The birthplace of the lucky cat.

hook: A wall painting marking where Tokyo's first skyscraper stood until an earthquake snapped it in half
facts: The Ryounkaku tower rose here in 1890, 12 brick storeys and 68.5 metres tall
It held Japan's first electric elevator, shut down after about six months for safety
The Great Kanto earthquake of 1923 destroyed it; engineers dynamited the remains
A reproduction of an 1890 Utagawa Kunimasa IV print of the tower now hangs on a building near the site
w: Tokyo's first skyscraper rose here in 1890, twelve brick storeys with the country's first electric lift, hawked as the Japanese Eiffel Tower. The novelty curdled fast; most climbed it once. The 1923 quake snapped it in two and engineers dynamited the rest. A wall mural of the old tower now marks the gap it left.

hook: A restaurant serving the same Edo street dish, simmered pond loach, since 1801
facts: Founded in 1801 and run by successive generations of the same family
Specialises in dozeu: whole loach simmered in a shallow iron pan over coals
The dish was cheap, energy-boosting fast food for Edo's working class
Seating is traditional tatami; the building has burned and been rebuilt repeatedly
w: Founded in 1801, this Asakusa institution still serves one thing: dozeu, whole pond loach simmered in a shallow iron pan over coals, scattered with mountains of spring onion. Edo street food for peddlers spending the morning's coppers. Fire took the building again and again; each time it rebuilt and served the same fish.

hook: A torii that survived for decades on the edge of an airfield because workers feared its curse
facts: Belonged to the old Anamori Inari Shrine, donated as a vermilion torii in 1929
Left standing on the airfield's edge for years after villagers were cleared post-war
Relocated to Tenkubashi by the riverbank in 1999, taking two days
Now renamed the Haneda Peace Torii, where visitors ring a bell and pray for peace
legend: They say attempts to remove it caused a rope to snap and injure workers, and the project director then sickened and died, convincing many it was cursed
w: When Haneda's airfield expanded, workers tried felling this vermilion torii of the old Anamori Inari shrine. The rope snapped and injured them; the director sickened and died. They called it cursed and left it standing on the runway's edge for decades, until 1999, when locals finally moved it whole to the riverbank. Now a peace gate.

hook: A tiny upstairs shrine that enshrines cats instead of foxes, with a strange octopus taboo
facts: Tucked on an upper floor beside the Toraya Akasaka sweet shop
Unusually, cats are enshrined here rather than the foxes of most Inari shrines
Registered as a Buddhist rather than Shinto institution; incense may be lit
Petitioners are told they must not eat octopus, with no explanation given
legend: One account holds the shrine honours a cat named Miki who repeatedly saved its owner from fire and disaster
w: Up a stairwell beside a famous sweet shop hides a shrine where cats, not the usual foxes, sit guard, lit by incense and registered oddly as Buddhist. They say a cat named Miki saved its owner from fire and worse. One rule for petitioners endures with no explanation offered: you must never eat octopus.

hook: Three thousand kites crammed above an old Western-style restaurant by its kite-mad chef
facts: Founded in 1977 by Shingo Modegi, first chef of the Taimeiken restaurant below
Collection numbers around 3,000 kites, with roughly 300 on display at a time
Holdings range from giant Edo battle kites to palm-sized scraps from around the world
Modegi flew a Japanese kite over Paris in 1965; the museum sits above the diner
w: Above his Western-style diner in Nihonbashi, the chef Shingo Modegi stashed a lifelong obsession: some three thousand kites, Edo warriors and gods painted on paper, palm-sized scraps, giant battle kites. He once flew a Japanese kite over startled Paris in 1965. Climb past the omelette rice to a cramped room hung with sky.

hook: The grave of the wandering writer who collected Japan's ghost stories and became Japanese
facts: Greek-born, Irish-raised Lafcadio Hearn came to Japan in 1890 and took the name Koizumi Yakumo
He gathered the ghost tales published as Kwaidan and many books on Japan
Buried in nonsectarian Zoshigaya Cemetery with his wife Setsu and son Kazuo
His plot is marked number 25 on the cemetery's visitor map
w: Greek-born, Irish-raised, half-blind in one eye, Lafcadio Hearn drifted to Japan in 1890, took the name Koizumi Yakumo, married into a samurai family and gathered the ghost stories that became Kwaidan. He lies here under his Japanese name in nonsectarian Zoshigaya, a man of three worlds in one quiet plot.

hook: A temple founded for the unclaimed dead that also birthed professional sumo
facts: Founded in 1657 to mourn the roughly 100,000 dead of the Great Meireki fire
Buried the unclaimed, including criminals, the drowned, and animals
Charity sumo bouts held here from 1768 are the origin of today's professional sport
The Chikara-zuka, or power mound, was raised in 1937 to honour past wrestlers
legend: Animal burials are said to have begun with a beloved pet cat of the shogun who founded the temple
w: Built in 1657 to mourn the hundred thousand dead of the great Meireki fire, this Ryogoku temple buried anyone unclaimed: criminals, the drowned, and animals, beginning with a shogun's cat. Charity sumo bouts held here from 1768 gave birth to the professional sport. A power mound still honours the old wrestlers.

hook: A museum to two former state monopolies, run by the cigarette company itself
facts: Pairs tobacco and salt because both were once Japanese government monopolies
Operated by Japan Tobacco; opened 1978 in Shibuya, moved to Sumida in 2015
Holds around 38,000 artefacts across salt and tobacco floors
Salt highlights include a 1.4-tonne rock-salt block and a 2-metre saint carved from Polish mine salt
w: Two things the Japanese state once monopolised, tobacco and salt, share a museum run by the cigarette company itself. The leaf gets the glory; the salt floor counters with a 1.4-tonne block of rock salt and a two-metre saint carved from Polish mine salt. Note how the health warnings appear on one floor and not the other.

hook: A shrine to entertainers and chancers wedged between Kabukicho and Golden Gai
facts: Founded in the mid-17th century, now hemmed in by Shinjuku's nightlife
Treated as a power spot for success in entertainment and business
A side path leads through a small tunnel of red torii to Itoku Inari
Sits immediately beside Kabukicho and the drinking alleys of Golden Gai
w: Wedged between Kabukicho's neon and the drinking warren of Golden Gai, this 17th-century shrine guards the entertainers and chancers of Shinjuku, its grounds tied to success on stage and in business. Slip to the side and a tunnel of small red torii leads to the fox spirit Itoku Inari. A power spot for the night trade.
